questions.q: What is the lower bound for comparison-based sorting?
Why can no comparison sort beat O(n log n) in the worst case?
questions.a: Comparison sorts must distinguish among n! possible orders, which requires logarithmically many decisions per item on average.

questions.q: When should you reach for a non-comparison sort?
questions.a: When keys are bounded integers or the input distribution lets counting or radix sort exploit structure.

questions.q: What does it mean for a sort to be stable?
questions.a: Equal keys keep their original relative order.

questions.q: Why does stability matter in practice?
questions.a: It lets you sort by one key and then refine by another without destroying the earlier ordering.

questions.q: When is stability more important than raw speed?
questions.a: When later processing depends on the original order of equal elements or on multi-key sorts.

questions.q: What is the average-case complexity of quicksort?
Why can quicksort degrade to O(n^2)?
questions.a: Bad pivot choices can keep splitting the array into very uneven parts.

questions.q: When is quicksort a strong choice?
questions.a: When you want fast in-place sorting on average and can control pivot quality.

questions.q: What is the extra space cost of mergesort?

questions.a: It merges two already ordered halves while preserving the order of equal elements.

questions.q: When is mergesort a better choice than quicksort?
questions.a: When you need stability or guaranteed O(n log n) worst-case behavior.

questions.q: What is the worst-case complexity of heapsort?
Why is heapsort usually considered in-place?
questions.a: It uses the array itself as the heap and needs only constant extra memory.

questions.q: When would you choose heapsort over quicksort or mergesort?
questions.a: When you want worst-case guarantees with low extra memory and do not need stability.

questions.q: What kind of data do counting and radix sort need?
questions.a: Bounded or structured keys, usually integers or fixed-length digits.

questions.q: Why can counting sort beat comparison sorts?
questions.a: It skips pairwise comparisons and counts key frequencies directly.

questions.q: When does counting or radix sort stop being attractive?
questions.a: When the key range is too large, memory gets wasteful, or the keys are not structured enough.
